observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to enjoin the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile the Actual Amounts of the Capital Outlay as presented in the SCBAA, Statement of Financial Position (SFPo), SCF and NFS, and to disclose any material matter that could support the presented PPE accounts in the financial statements. Consequently, effect the adjustments or corrections made in the financial statements.
In their letter reply, Management explained that the Capital Outlay reported in the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) does not include continuing appropriations for Capital Outlay. The other variances noted represent non-cash transactions that led to an increase in Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE), primarily due to the recognition of PPE items paid in the previous year and the transfer of certain assets from the Trust Fund, also originating from prior-year transactions.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant commented that all necessary adjustments and corrections will be appropriately reflected in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to:
Reconcile the Non-Tax Revenues and the Budget and Actual Amounts of the 20% LDF and LDRRMF with the Statement of Financial Performance and Utilization Reports, respectively, and prepare corrected reports;
Appropriately present the reconciling items and ensure that the amounts reconcile with corroborating records and reports to support the accuracy of the figures presented; and
Reconcile the Capital Outlay with the Statement of Financial Performance, supported by schedules that substantiate the amounts presented.
The Municipal Accountant explained that the variance under Service Income pertains to Garbage Fee collections, which were classified under Business Income in the Statement of Financial Performance, in accordance with Section 37 of the Local Government Accounting Manual (LGAM). Furthermore, the 20% Development Fund and the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) will be reconciled with their respective utilization reports, with adequate disclosures and supporting schedules to be provided accordingly.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to recognize the appropriate Semi-Expendable Expenses account for semi-expendable items purchased and issued during CY 2024;
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the adjusting entries to derecognize semi-expendable items purchased prior to CY 2024, which were initially recognized under the Property, Plant, and Equipment accounts.  Ensure that the carrying amount of the items is expensed/charged to the Prior Period Adjustment, and the corresponding accumulated depreciation and accumulated impairment losses are closed in the books of accounts. The proposed adjusting entries are as follows;
To recognize adjustments due to a change in accounting policy on capitalization threshold:
Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain the Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card (SPLC); and
Instruct the GSO, Inventory Committee, and Accountable Officer to prepare and maintain the forms, registries, and reports, except the SPLC, to enhance accountability and internal control over semi-expendable property.
Management commented that the Municipal Accountant will prepare the necessary adjusting entries to properly recognize semi-expendable items as expenses and to derecognize those acquired prior to Calendar Year 2024 that were previously recorded under Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE). In addition, both the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Office (GSO) have committed to maintaining the required forms, registries, and reports to strengthen accountability and improve internal controls over semi-expendable properties.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
Make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the misstatement, using the following pro-forma entries:
Record the payments for insurance premiums as prepayments under the Prepaid Insurance accounts and recognize the monthly expiration of the prepayments under the insurance expense to reflect the accurate balances in the financial reports.
Management concurred with the recommendation to make the necessary adjusting entries. Given that some insurance premiums have already expired as of the reporting date, the Insurance Expense account will be debited in addition to the Prepaid Insurance account in accordance with proper accounting treatment. These adjustments will result in a reconciling item in the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts (SCBAA), as the Budget Office had fully obligated the amount for payment regardless of the insurance coverage period.

We reiterated our recommendation from last year that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer, take the following actions:
Require the GSO and the Office of the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain complete SCs and SLCs, respectively, and regularly reconcile their records to ensure that all purchases and issuances are properly accounted for;
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct a comprehensive physical count of all types of inventory items semiannually, prepare the RPCI, and submit it to the Auditor concerned no later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively. Also, invite a representative from the COA to serve as a witness during the physical count; and
Instruct the GSO to prepare the weekly SSMI based on the original RIS submitted by the various offices responsible for the custody of these inventories, submit the same to the Municipal Accountant, and submit the Report on Supplies and Materials Issued to the Audit Team quarterly.
The Management stated in their letter reply that the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Office (GSO) have agreed to implement the recommendations by updating the Supplies Ledger Cards (SLCs) and Stock Cards (SCs) accordingly. Additionally, they have requested additional qualified personnel to be assigned to the maintenance of these records.
The Physical Inventory Team will resume the conduct of a semi-annual physical count of all inventory items to facilitate the preparation of the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI), which shall be submitted to the Auditor no later than July 31 and January 31 of each year. Additionally, the GSO will prepare the Weekly Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) based on the original Requisition and Issue Slips (RIS) submitted by the various offices responsible for the custody and use of inventory items.

We reiterated our recommendation from last year that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the General Services Officer to take appropriate action in maintaining Property Cards for all the PPEs of the LGU;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain Property Ledger Cards and reconcile them accordingly with the Property Cards maintained by GSO;
Begin the process of complying with the One-Time Cleansing of PPE account balances to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the assertions regarding the existence, condition, and valuation of the PPE accounts.
We further recommended that upon reorganization of the Property and Equipment Inventory Committee (PEIC) the Local Chief Executive ensure that:
A physical count of all properties will be conducted at least once a year to prepare the RPCPPE to be submitted to the Audit Team; and
Unserviceable properties of the Municipality will be inspected to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable. Properties (IRRUP) and the Waste Materials Report (WMR) to be submitted to the Audit Team.
The Management commented that the Physical Inventory Team has been instructed to carry out a one-time cleansing of inventory records. Additionally, they have requested the assignment of additional qualified personnel to assist in encoding and recording the related documents, a process estimated to take approximately six to ten months to complete.
Furthermore, in compliance with the applicable rules and regulations on the disposal of public property, all unserviceable items are scheduled to be formally disposed of within the third quarter of 2025.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Coordinate with the Provincial Assessor’s Office, with the assistance of the Municipal Assessor to determine the appraised value of the fifteen (15) parcels of land, which will be the basis for recording in the books of accounts;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to determine the amount of the donated lot to PNP to be derecognized in the books of accounts in coordination with the Municipal Assessor; and
Direct the Ad Hoc Committee to comply with the submitted 3-year action plan and to process the titling of all parcels of land owned by the Municipal Government, including the completion and submission of all the lacking documentary requirements to facilitate faster processing of transfer of ownership and titling under the name of the Municipal Government.
In their response letter, Management stated that initial steps have already been undertaken to commence the titling process for the lots.
They also noted that the appraised value of the 15 parcels of land will be determined based on their market value, as supported by the Certified True Copies issued by the Municipal Assessor.
Furthermore, Management has filed a petition with the Provincial Legal Office for the declaration of loss of title, in order to facilitate the appraisal of the parcel of land donated to the Philippine National Police (PNP).
Lastly, Management affirmed their commitment to comply with the requirements of the submitted three-year plan for the issuance of special patents for the untitled properties.

In view of the noted deficiencies, we recommended that Management:
Ensure the APP is complete and accurate, encompassing goods, infrastructure projects, services, and all essential information, to preserve its ongoing relevance and utility as a planning and control tool;
Direct all department heads to thoroughly plan their procurement activities and to include in their PPMPs all necessary items for their office, along with planned programs, projects, and activities for the upcoming budget year, in order to avoid or minimize revisions to the consolidated APP; and
Direct all department heads to submit any updates or revisions of their respective PPMPs to the BAC for inclusion in the adjusted APP.
The Management averred to the recommendations and commented that they had taken steps in the 1st quarter of 2025, where consolidation of PPMPs is observed. The BAC agreed to continue and abide by this process for a sound procurement undertaking. The BAC will observe the cause for an initiative to remind various departments to observe the timelines in preparing and submitting the same.

We recommended Management of the following:
Direct the BAC to strictly comply with the requirement that procurement reports be submitted to the GPPB and furnish the Audit Team with a copy of each report, duly supported by the corresponding auto-generated acknowledgment received from the GPPB; and
Ensure that all BAC members and relevant staff are fully informed of the required procurement forms, deadlines, and guidelines, possibly through regular training sessions and workshops.
The BAC Chairman affirmed that they will exert utmost effort to ensure full compliance with the provisions of Sections 7.4 and 12.2 of Republic Act No. 9184.

We recommended that Management discontinue the use of reimbursements for procurements such as meals, supplies, repairs, and donations, and instead process payments directly to suppliers through checks, in line with Section 2 of COA Circular No. 97-002. Exceptions may be allowed only for urgent or petty expenses where check issuance is impractical, provided that proper documentation and justification are submitted to support the necessity of such reimbursements and prevent their misuse as a regular procurement method.
Management issued a memorandum directing all office and department heads, as well as requesting personnel, to submit their proposed programs, projects, and activities (PPAs) on or before the 5th day of the last month of each quarter. The submission must include a well-planned list of required supplies, materials, meals, and other logistical needs, along with the estimated quantity and the number of participants or beneficiaries, whether for consumption or distribution. These details must be aligned with the scheduled implementation of the PPA and submitted in coordination with the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat. To ensure proper monitoring and coordination, a focal person will be designated to oversee the Calendar of Activities and ensure that all PPAs are well-planned and properly communicated.
Management further clarified that, in cases where check issuance is deemed impractical, a written justification must be provided and attached as part of the supporting documents prior to allowing reimbursements through the Petty Cash Fund. Such reimbursements will remain subject to applicable withholding taxes, particularly in instances involving emergency needs or unavoidable circumstances.

In light of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Direct the BAC and TWG to thoroughly verify and validate the Statement of All Ongoing Government and Private Contracts, including contracts awarded but not yet started, during the post-qualification, ensuring that contracts are awarded only to eligible and qualified bidders; and
Automatically disqualify bidders whose submitted eligibility documents are proven to be misrepresented.
The BAC Chairman, together with the BAC members and the TWG, convened a meeting to address the observation. During the meeting, the TWG Head and its members were instructed to strictly comply with the provisions of Sections 34.3 and 34.8 of the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management:
Implement Paragraph 4.2(g) of Appendix 17 of the IRR for RA 9184;
Ensure proper verification of the Statement of Work Accomplished submitted by the contractor and require attachment of complete geo-tagged photos to the Disbursement Vouchers before processing payment; and
Furthermore, to enhance transparency, accountability, and accuracy in the inspection and validation process, it is recommended that the LGU establish an independent inspectorate team, separate from the Office of the Municipal Engineer, to increase reliance on the verification of completion of infrastructure projects.
The Management’s Inspectorate Team conducted a site inspection and promptly notified the contractor to address the reported issues concerning the findings during the inspection process. Audit Team conducted a revalidation, and the project was already completed and complied with the recommendations.
During the Exit Conference, the Management commented that they will observe strict compliance with the recommendations and committed to ensuring proper verification of Statement of Work Accomplished before processing the payment.

We recommended that Management:
Implement a more rigorous and systematic verification process for all “As-Built” plans before approval. This should include cross-referencing the submitted plans with actual work completed and requiring site inspections by OME.
A detailed cost assessment should be carried out to determine the full extent of the unperformed works in each project.
To enhance transparency, accountability, and accuracy in the inspection and validation process, it is recommended that the LGU establish an independent inspectorate team, separate from the Office of the Municipal Engineer, to increase reliance on the verification of completion of infrastructure projects.
Require recoupment of the cost of unperformed works from the Contractors.
The Management’s Inspectorate Team conducted a site inspection and promptly notified the contractor to take corrective action on the identified issues observed during the inspection.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
Based on the Audit Team’s revalidation in March 2025, it was found that the Revised As-Built Plans had yet to be submitted and several discrepancies remain unresolved. The details are as follows:

We recommended that Management do the following:
Direct the BAC and the BAC Secretariat, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, to take immediate action to address the noted deficiencies and ensure that all lacking documents identified in the auditorial review are submitted;
Instruct the BAC and the BAC Secretariat, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to ensure compliance with the documentary requirements specified in COA Circular 2012-001. This will enable the Audit Team to thoroughly evaluate projects and establish the validity, legitimacy, and propriety of the transaction.
The Management, through the BAC, expressed its commitment to strictly adhere to the provisions of the IRR of RA 9184 and all relevant circulars issued by the appropriate authorities.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly enforce the submission of warranty security by the suppliers or, in its absence, to deduct the required retention money from payments to answer any manufacturing defects after the delivery of goods. This will uphold the Municipality’s right to claim remedies against defective goods and help avoid unnecessary expenses that could otherwise burden the Municipality.
The Management stated that they strictly enforce the imposition of Warranty and Retention Bonds for Infrastructure Projects. However, in the procurement of goods, suppliers only submitted a signed Warranty After-Sales Transaction document, indicating that the delivered items are covered by a one-year warranty against defects.
To strengthen compliance with warranty requirements, Retention Money has since been incorporated into the procurement checklist for goods as a standard control measure. Additionally, the Municipal Accountant submitted a Journal Entry Voucher confirming that Retention Money was deducted in the current year in cases where the supplier did not submit a warranty bond.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the BPLO, to devise measures to validate the declared gross receipts of establishments applying for permits to engage in business, especially those with direct transactions with the LGU and its barangays.
Enforce the collection of underpayments, including the applicable surcharges and interest as provided in the Municipal Revenue Code. Non-compliance may necessitate the issuance of a corresponding Notice of Charge.
Refrain from issuing Mayor’s Permit to and entering into contract with noted LGU suppliers with unpaid tax obligations.
Include in the Instruction to Bidders or other documents issued by the Bids and Awards Committee that prospective suppliers/bidders should have no unpaid obligation with the Municipality as an eligibility requirement. A certification by the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant must be issued to effect this requirement.
Management commented that the Office of the Municipal Treasurer has taken immediate steps to implement the necessary corrective actions, such as verification and validation, notice issuance, collection measures, eligibility requirements for bidders, and documentation reporting.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that additional cash advance shall not be granted to an accountable officer when the previous cash advance remains unliquidated;
Require the Municipal Accounting Office to use the appropriate Advances Accounts as provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts in the recording of cash advances; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to release allowances and personnel benefits of regular employees through their payroll ATM.
The Municipal Treasurer has requested the designation of additional bonded Special Disbursing Officers to facilitate cash advances for the LGU’s special purpose and time-bound undertakings. The Accounting Office begun using the appropriate accounts to record such transactions. Furthermore, benefits for regular employees, such as RATA, Cellcard allowance for SB Officials, and hazard pay, subsistence, and laundry allowances for employees of the MHO and MSWD, along with other future benefits, will be disbursed through the payroll ATM. The potential implementation of payroll ATM for all personnel under the Contract of Services will be coordinated with the Human Resource Management Officer for possible endorsement and enrollment with the Land Bank Servicing Office.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure timely liquidation of cash advances in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 and to avoid a similar scenario.
In cases where cash advances are not liquidated within the prescribed guidelines, it is recommended that Management take appropriate action to ensure the immediate liquidation of long-standing cash advances. Such actions may include withholding salaries and other amounts due from the concerned personnel, particularly after they have failed to comply with a demand for liquidation.
The Management commented that notices had been issued to the concerned employees regarding the temporary withholding of their salaries and other benefits, pending the full liquidation of their outstanding cash advances. It was further confirmed that their salaries and bonuses have already been withheld in line with this directive.
During the exit conference, Management informed the audit team that one of the accountable officers had already fully settled the unliquidated cash advances. Meanwhile, the other accountable officer submitted an appeal requesting partial settlement of the amount due through deductions from his withheld salaries, with the remaining balance to be paid in cash.

We recommended that management:
Strictly prohibit the use of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund for items that are not allowed to be charged against the fund, in compliance with the provisions of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013, as such actions result in improper use of funds that may warrant the issuance of a Notice of Disallowance.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) enjoin the LDRRMO to reassess the necessity of pursuing the unimplemented PPAs; otherwise, reprogram those PPAs towards initiatives that strengthen disaster resilience and ensure maximum fund utilization.
The Management commented that the various expenses were interpreted as related expenses under the MDRRM Plan, Programs, Projects, and Activities.  Furthermore, they also averred that if these were observed to be unrelated, the Management agreed not to repeat the charging of them.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
The Audit Team finds it relevant to emphasize that many of these expenses do not fall within the four thematic areas outlined in Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1, issued jointly by the NDRRMC, DBM, and DILG on March 25, 2013. Except for office supplies explicitly used for the production of brochures and flyers intended for public distribution aimed at risk mitigation, most administrative expenses were found to be unrelated to the thematic areas of preparedness and mitigation.
In addition, regarding the ₱1,230,534.11 disbursed for salaries of Job Order (JO) personnel, the Audit Team noted that only a portion of the amount was paid to JOs under the MDRRM Office whose responsibilities included flood monitoring and the cleaning and clearing of rivers—activities considered essential to disaster preparedness. However, other JOs assigned to departments outside the MDRRM Office were found to be performing functions not directly aligned with disaster preparedness or mitigation efforts.
To ensure adherence to budgetary and programmatic guidelines, it is recommended that such unrelated expenditures should not be charged to the MDRRM Fund.

We recommended the Management the following:
To conduct a comprehensive review of the projects, programs, and activities (PPAs) proposed for funding under 20% Development Fund to prevent the inclusion of such PPAs that do not promote socio-economic and environmental development and other expenditure items that do not directly benefit the general welfare of the people and those not allowed specifically under the provisions of JMC No. 1 of DBM, DOF and DILG as these do not directly support the purpose for which the fund should be appropriated and utilized; and
To stop using the 20% Development Fund for expenditures that are prohibited under the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 dated November 4, 2020, as this can lead to irregular expenditures and may result in the issuance of a Notice of Disallowance.
The Management commented that the purpose of the purchased aircon is to maintain the hardware/computer equipment in its normal/lowest temperature as possible to avoid overheating of equipment, protection and longevity of servers and ICT hardware, heat regulation for ICT equipment, temperature control for server environment, systems failure, sectoral data loss and downtime of servers that may lead to service interruption; inefficient governance; and adverse effect on public service delivery without proper temperature control. The aircon, though not directly an IT equipment, supports the Local ICT Infrastructure and, for supplementary engineering activities, also pertains to the ventilation and effective maintenance of local digital governance of ICT Equipment.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
While the purchase of the air-conditioning unit may indirectly support the promotion of development and general welfare, Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1 clearly identifies such expenditures as ineligible for funding. Specifically, the JMC provides that the purchase, maintenance, or repair of administrative office furniture, fixtures, equipment, or appliances—including air-conditioning units—shall not be charged against the fund. Accordingly, this expense is considered non-allowable under the provisions of the said circular.

We recommended that the Management:
Cease charging non-gender-responsive expenses to the fund allocated for Gender and Development initiatives; and
Instruct the GFPS-TWG to take proactive measures to ensure proper utilization and implementation of the approved GAD Plan and Budget, in strict compliance with the above provisions to address more gender-related issues and contribute to realizing the country’s commitment to strengthening women’s empowerment, promoting gender equality, and fostering gender-inclusive development.
The Management commented that these disbursements were chargeable under the GAD Fund due to equal opportunities for men and women to avail themselves of scholarship allowances to augment their high school and College expenses. A summary of the Municipal Scholars specifying their gender to present the number of scholars by gender was submitted as an attachment to the payroll forms.
Further, the Management emphasized that the payments of salaries and wages of Job Order and Hired Employees were chargeable under GAD since they help and report exclusively for the updating of the GAD Database per office. However, their main role to update the database in their respective offices was not specified in their functions during the last quarter of the year.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
While Management interpreted the provision of scholarship as a valid expense chargeable against the GAD Fund, the Audit Team emphasized that the initiative primarily addresses educational concerns and does not directly respond to any gender-related issue, as required under GAD guidelines.
With regard to the payment of salaries for Job Order (JO) personnel and hired employees, the Audit Team conducted a verification of their reported accomplishments based on the accomplishment reports attached to the payroll. The review revealed that the majority of the reported tasks were not related to the updating or maintenance of the GAD Database, thereby calling into question the appropriateness of charging such expenses to the GAD Fund.

To ensure that the financial assistance granted is valid, regular, and reasonable, we recommended that Management:
Formulate and issue a policy or guidelines for the implementation of the AICS program, detailing who is entitled to assistance, the amount to be provided based on the recipient's current situation, the number of times the financial assistance can be received by the same individual in a year, and the necessary documentary requirements; and
Formalize these policies by issuing an Executive Order, memorandum, or equivalent document that will provide guidance for the implementation of AICs.
Management explained that the provision of financial aid through the AICS Program is compliant with the provisions of Memorandum Circular No. 15, Series of 2022, issued by the DSWD National Office, since they did not exceed the maximum rate as prescribed. The said memorandum provides updated guidelines for the implementation of AICS, encompassing various forms of support such as medical, funeral, transportation, educational, food, and other cash assistance, including emergency shelter aid.
It was further noted that families who received Emergency Shelter Assistance amounting to ₱20,000.00 were subjected to a comprehensive evaluation of their individual circumstances by MSWD staff and social workers. These evaluations were duly justified and approved by the Local Chief Executive (LCE). Such action is aligned with the provisions of Section 28 of Republic Act No. 7279, which requires LGUs to provide financial support to displaced families in cases where relocation cannot be undertaken.

We reiterated our recommendation from last year that Management, through its Sangguniang Bayan, ensure expeditious processing of the review and approval of the revised Revenue Code. This will yield new/additional revenue sources supporting the Municipality's programs/projects/activities, which will be advantageous for the community's members.
The Sangguniang Bayan passed and approved Resolution No. 2024-49, dated March 26, 2024, a resolution requesting Hon. Bona Fe De Vera-Parayno to establish a committee tasked with studying, revisiting, and recommending amendments to the Revenue Code of the Municipality of Mangaldan. In line with this, Management has already initiated preliminary steps toward revising the Revenue Code and is committed to utilizing the remaining months of 2025 to expedite and complete its comprehensive updating and revision.

We recommended that Management:
Execute an order stating guidelines for implementing the prescribed net take-home pay for all municipal employees;
Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Head of Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) to review loan accommodations and other payroll deductions of the Municipal employees before allowing them, ensuring that they do not cause their monthly net take-home pay to fall below the prescribed ceiling of ₱5,000.00; and
Instruct those in charge of payroll preparation to exercise diligence in monitoring the net take-home pay of Municipal to ensure compliance with the requirements of existing law.
The Management, in coordination with the Human Resource Management Office (HRMO), addressed the observation by informing affected employees, particularly those whose Net Take Home Pay (NTHP) falls below the prescribed minimum, of the potential non deduction of their loan repayments to authorized lending agencies. This situation stems from non-compliance with the general provisions of Section 56 of Republic Act No. 11975. These employees will be required to settle their obligations directly with the concerned agencies. They will also be informed of the possible consequences, including the accrual of interest and penalties, and the suspension of loan availment or renewal privileges.
Moreover, the HRMO and the Municipal Accountant will no longer issue loan certifications to employees who do not meet the minimum NTHP requirement, unless they comply with the applicable policies. This is in line with Executive Order No. 2023-023, which authorizes the processing of loan applications for regular employees with accredited financial institutions, provided that a minimum NTHP of ₱5,000.00 is maintained.
In light of this, Management respectfully appeals for the consideration of allowing deductions for employees with an NTHP below ₱5,000.00, on the condition that their future loan availments or renewals will be closely monitored.

We recommended that Management:
Provide an exclusive service vehicle for the Real Property Tax Section to aid in their logistical needs.
Develop a collection plan for tax accounts overdue by more than five years and make necessary adjustments in the books if these are deemed uncollectible.
The Management commented, through the Municipal Treasurer, that they have initiated several strategies to enhance the collection of delinquent Real Property Tax (RPT) and Special Education Tax (SET) receivables. These measures include the issuance of Notices of Delinquency, public posting of the names of delinquent taxpayers in conspicuous locations, dissemination of information regarding the imposition of penalties and interest to encourage prompt payment, as well as the use of tarpaulins, flyers, and social media platforms to raise public awareness.
Additionally, Management stated that a service vehicle will be assigned to the Treasury Office to support field collection activities and improve the efficiency of revenue collection efforts.

We recommended that Management to completely desist from the practice of purchasing goods, and reimbursement of travel expenses that are contemplated under the law as unnecessary and unconscionable as they could be characterized as personal in nature and do not benefit the welfare of the general public; or are not essential in the efficient and effective performance of the Local Government Unit.
Non-compliance herein may warrant the issuance of Notice of Disallowance pursuant to COA Circular 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.
The Management stated in their letter reply that the procurement of Polo Barong and Filipiniana uniforms was the first and last, and no similar purchases are planned in the future.
During the exit conference, Management explained that the LGU’s high-end equipment for documentation purposes remains underutilized due to the absence of a qualified technical expert. As a result, the LGU opted to outsource documentation services. In response, Management acknowledged the need to invest in skilled personnel to optimize the use of existing equipment moving forward and committed that the same will not be repeated again.
In their response letter, Management justified that the expenses incurred for the State of the Municipality Address (SOMA) were in accordance with the mandate of the Local Government Code, classifying the activity as part of the LGU’s administrative functions and a legitimate annual undertaking. As a public governance and financial performance reporting initiative, the SOMA serves as a key mechanism for promoting transparency and accountability. It also provides a platform to present key performance indicators and engage various sectors of the community. Accordingly, all related expenses were incurred with due regard for appropriateness, necessity, prudence, compliance, good faith, absence of personal gain, and a commitment to remedial measures where applicable.

We recommended that Management:
Continue to insure the properties of the Municipal Government with the GSIS, and require the GSO, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to further identify the properties that need to be insured by preparing the Property Inventory Form (PIF)  and submitting it to the GSIS as the basis for the assessment of general insurance coverage for all insurable assets, to provide a safeguard against substantial losses in the event of damage to, or loss of, properties caused by fortuitous events;
Submit a complete inventory of insurable properties to GSIS for them to make an appropriate evaluation of whether the items included can be covered. The annual report must also be submitted to the Audit Team; and
Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to provide an appropriation for the payment of premiums for all insurable properties of the Municipal Government.
In their letter reply, Management stated that the Inventory Committee, in coordination with the General Services Office (GSO) and the Municipal Accountant, will prepare the Property Inventory Form for submission to the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) to facilitate the assessment of general insurance coverage for all insurable assets. Should funds be available, the corresponding insurance premiums will be processed and paid to GSIS. Additionally, the Municipal Budget Officer will ensure the appropriation of insurance premiums under the Annual Budget for Fiscal Year 2026.

We recommended that Management:
Evaluate the necessity of Job Order workers rendering services even on weekends and ensure that the contracts include the number of working days per month and number of working hours per day to establish the legality of future claims.
Secure an authority to render overtime and a quantified overtime accomplishment report to support payment of working days exceeding the calendar days and adjustment of wages.
Ensure that the Time Book and Payroll, Daily Time Record, and Accomplishment Reports have a consistent number of actual workdays before processing wage payments.
Refrain from charging payments of salaries and wages of Job Order and hired service workers to LDRRM and GAD funds or any other funds not appropriated for such obligations.
Management stated in their letter reply that, effective immediately, all Job Order (JO) employees shall be entitled to one day off per week. Under no circumstances shall any JO employee render work for seven consecutive days, except when justified by a valid and reasonable cause.
Furthermore, the payroll cut-off periods for JO employees shall be observed on the 1st and 16th of each month. This measure is intended to ensure accurate accounting for the periods covering the 1st to 15th and 16th to the end of the month. No payroll adjustments shall be entertained outside of these designated cut-off periods.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure the proper recording and reconciliation of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts to reflect accurate amounts in the financial statements.
Require the Municipal Treasurer to issue official receipt to acknowledge the receipt of checks received from one fund to another as basis of the Municipal Accountant in the preparation of a journal entry voucher for proper recording in the books of accounts.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Accountant to include additional disclosures as to the total cash payments for acquisition of long-term assets/ PPEs. Moreover, it is essential to present the effects of transactions from the Notes to FS pertaining to PPEs to the Statement of Cash Flows and to Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts in a comparable and understandable manner.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to review the errors noted in the Cash Receipts Journal and make the necessary corrections.
Require the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that collections for Property Taxes are equally recorded under Basic and SEF Columns and review entries at the end of each month.
Explain the discrepancy of debited Deferred Real Property Tax and Deferred Special Education Tax and the amount of collections reported in the Abstract of RPT Collections from Treasury, and record necessary corrections.
Recognize the realized property taxes from the advanced payments in 2022.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate and retrieve the necessary supporting documents of the receivable and liability accounts totaling ₱5,528,038.33 to determine the details of the receivable accounts and to identify those who are responsible for settling these obligations/debts with the LGU in order to establish the validity and accuracy of the respective receivable account balances presented in the financial statements; and
Coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer in collecting the Due from Officers and Employees amounting to ₱335,405.00 through the issuance of demand letters to identified employees and officers and require them to settle their obligations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Coordinate with the Provincial Assessor’s Office with the assistance of the Municipal Assessor to determine the appraised value of the twenty-one (21) parcels of land which will be the basis in recording in the books of accounts.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to determine the amount of the donated lot to PNP to be derecognized in the books of accounts in coordination with the Municipal Assessor.
Require the Ad Hoc Committee to prepare an action plan in obtaining Transfer Certificate of Titles (TCTs) in the name of the Municipal Government of Mangaldan for the twenty-one (21) untitled lots pursuant to Section 39 of PD No. 1445 in order to protect the interest of the LGU and to avoid future potential disputes.
Instruct the Ad Hoc Committee to legally document the ownership of untitled lots by obtaining a TCT in the name of the Municipal Government of Mangaldan pursuant to Section 39 of PD No. 1445 in order to protect the interest of the LGU and to avoid future potential disputes.
Include in the annual budget the needed amount for the initiative to obtain land titles on lands that the LGU asserts ownership.

We recommended Management the following:
To require the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.
For the Accounting Department to keep and maintain ledger and subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network using the prescribed format as mentioned in the above circular.
For the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
For the General Services Officer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components also using the prescribed format as mentioned in the above circular.
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to monitor and follow up on the timely liquidation of cash advances by preparing and sending demand letters as needed.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that additional cash advance shall not be granted to an accountable officer when the previous cash advance remained unliquidated.
Require the Municipal Accounting Office to use the appropriate Advances Accounts as provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts in the recording of cash advances.
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to release allowances and personnel benefits of regular employees through their payroll ATM.

We recommended Management the following:
Payments for goods and services be made directly to suppliers or dealers through the issuance of checks, in compliance with Section 2 of COA Circular No. 97-002. Exceptions may apply in cases where the needed items are highly necessary and urgent or are petty in nature, requiring immediate payment, making payments through check impractical.
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to use the Petty Cash Fund for payments of petty expenses amounting to ₱2,000.00 and below.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer for immediate submission of the twelve (12) disbursement vouchers totaling ₱4,179,924.00.
We also recommended that Management hire additional personnel in the Accounting Office to address the voluminous workload and prevent the delayed submission of accounts.

We recommended that the Management, particularly the Bids and Awards Committee, ensure that the eligibility requirements of the suppliers particularly on the line of business to which they are authorize to engage must be similar or within the subject of the procurement so that the government can maximize its limited fund through procurement of high-quality products at competitive prices.

We recommended that the Management require the Bids and Awards Committee to:
Submit copies of the perfected contracts listed in Table 1 with its complete supporting documents.
Strictly comply in the submission of copies of perfected contracts, approved POs, and supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period of five (5) working days as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001 for CY 2024 projects. Administrative disciplinary action as provided in Section 4 of the same Circular against the concerned employee be imposed upon unjustified failure of compliance.
We also recommended that the Management assign additional staff in the BAC Secretariat Office who will be in-charge in the preparation of the documents for each completed contract for submission to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to strictly utilize the PCF for payment of authorized non-recurring petty operating or miscellaneous expenses which cannot be conveniently paid through checks; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer to pay AICS expenses through cash advances by the special disbursing officer responsible under her Office for the payment of the financial assistance and subsequent liquidation thereof. This is without prejudice to the intention of the Management to install other means of internal control that would adhere to the prescribed policy.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to record the cash advance for payments of AICS under the account Advances to Special Disbursing Officer and Credit to Cash account whenever payment is made. Further, ensure that these cash advances were liquidated accordingly with complete supporting documents and through the use of Disbursement Vouchers instead of Petty Cash Vouchers.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to review the computation provided in subsection (1) to (6) and require the concerned payees to refund any excess travel claims after verification.
Submit the required documents mentioned in Subsection (1) and (2)
Refrain from allowing claims of per diems and fare exceeding the rates allowed by EO 77.
Strictly comply with the provisions of NBC No. 563, dated April 22, 2016 issued by the DBM in the payment of registration fees for trainings/seminars.
It is also emphasized that in the event that trainings/seminars exceed the stipulated limit outlined in the NBC, the respective official will be responsible for covering the excess amount. The Municipality will solely be liable for the expenses explicitly specified within the circular.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Sangguniang Bayan to promptly revise and update their Local Revenue Code in accordance with the provisions of Section 191 of the Local Government Code. This action aims to ensure that it reflects current economic conditions and supports the Municipality’s financial sustainability.

We recommended that management:
Instruct the Municipal Scholarship Committee to retrieve and provide the files of the scholars who were paid in 2023 for further verification of the Audit Team to establish the propriety and validity of the payments made.
Direct the Municipal Scholarship Committee to separate the guidelines for high school and college scholars and to make revisions considering the noted deficiencies, in order to properly assess each applicant and ensure that the recipients are the rightful beneficiaries of the program;
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure complete documentation for all succeeding transactions related to the scholarship grant payments; and
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to prepare separate vouchers for the payment of scholarship grants to high school and college scholars to facilitate proper monitoring of grades submitted to the Municipal Scholarship Committee.

We recommended that Management:
Identify delinquent taxpayers who cannot be located and intensify management’s strategy to locate these taxpayers.
Determine their collection plan for taxes overdue by over 5 years and make necessary adjustments on their books if they are considered uncollectible.

We recommended that Management:
Require the GSO, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to prepare a duly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) to report all unserviceable properties of the LGU.
Create a Disposal/Appraisal Committee headed by the GSO to conduct and manage the disposal process which includes the preparation of appraisal reports and other required documents to facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with the procedures under Section 79 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that Management:
Consider including the future expenses for Pindang Festival supplies, such as those noted, as part of tourism expenses of the LGU instead of being used as a fund-raising project of the Mangaldan Pindang Festival Executive Committee.
Instruct the different heads of the LGU to refund the cost of grill packages reimbursed from the LGU fund.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that Driver’s Trip Tickets attached to the vouchers are duly accomplished and consistent with issued fuel slip.
Directly deposit the share of Barangays from RPT to the barangay accounts from the general fund.
Correct the noted misclassification of expense accounts in the restated comparative balance in the preparation of CY 2024 Financial Statement.
Evaluate the necessity of Job Order workers rendering services even on weekends and ensure that the contracts include the number of working days per month to establish the legality of future claims.

We recommended that the local government unit strictly refrain from charging non-gender-responsive expenses against the fund allocated for Gender and Development initiatives. The Local Chief Executive should take proactive measures to ensure the proper implementation of the approved GAD Plan and Budget, in strict compliance with the provisions outlined in Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA and the DILG Guidelines for Institutionalizing Gender Responsive in Local Governance in order to address more gender related issues and contribute to the realization of the country’s commitment to strengthening women’s empowerment, promoting gender equality, and fostering gender-inclusive development

We recommended that Management:
Exercise prudence in the reimbursement of meals served for the visitors of the Mayor’s Office by assessing its frequency and ensuring that funds are used solely for public purpose.
Ensure that expenses incurred are duly supported by complete documentations to validate claims.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of purchasing funeral wreaths for its deceased constituents as it takes the nature of a personal expense and does not benefit the welfare of the general public.
Non-compliance herein may warrant the issuance of Notice of Disallowance pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.

We recommended that management refrain from utilizing the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) for items that are not allowed to be charged against the fund in compliance with the provisions of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013, as the same results to irregular expenditures.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) enjoin the LDRRMO to implement the programmed PPAs in the LDRRMF for the maximum utilization of the fund.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the BPLO, to device measures to validate the declared gross receipts of establishments applying for permits to engage in business especially those who are having direct transactions with the LGU and its barangays.
Enforce the collection of the underpayment including the applicable surcharges and interests provided in the Municipal Revenue Code. Non-compliance may warrant the issuance of corresponding Notice of Charge.
Require the Municipal Treasurer to collect business taxes from outside contractors who engage in projects within the municipality applying the situs of taxation. In this regard, issue demand letters from these contractors or require them to pay the accrued business taxes with the municipal government before being allowed to participate in the bidding process.

We recommended that:
For the Office of the Municipal Engineer to issue certificate of final acceptance only after inspecting the projects and promptly correcting or repairing any noted deficiencies by the contractor. Additionally, conducting thorough inspections safeguards the quality and functionality of the completed projects, contributing to the overall success and reliability of the construction process.
For the Local School Board to assess proposed projects comprehensively, taking into account not only the initial construction aspects but also the long-term maintenance costs. This approach ensures smart decision-making, focusing on keeping the facilities in good condition.

We recommended Management the following:
For the Office of the Sangguniang Bayan to submit proof of the Revised Market Code’s submission, duly received by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan. Providing proof of submission shows commitment to transparency and adherence to procedural requirements.
Ensure the immediate implementation of the Revised Market Code following its approval by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan. This is for Management to have smooth process in regulating market activities, rental fees, and stallholder selection.
For the Office of the Municipal Engineer to demand repair works of the noted defects before issuance of certificate of final acceptance given that it is within the defects liability period to ensure that the project is completed to satisfaction.
For the Office of the Market Supervisor to maintain cleanliness of the facility and regularly inspect the property for any damages. Regular cleanliness maintenance ensures a hygenic, well-kept environment, and longevity of the property.
For the Municipal Health Officer to submit update on the utilization of the 2nd floor of the Municipal Birthing Clinic after repair works. This helps in assessing the effectiveness of the repairs and identifying any issues that need attention.

We recommended that Management adopt the procedures laid out in LBC No. 111, particularly the following:
Prepare a 5-year business plan, stressing on the importance of directing the LEE operations towards financial self-sufficiency;
Ensure the regular conduct of the SAFSSR and preparation of the SAFSSR report; and
Five years after instituting the necessary changes laid down in the BP, prepare the Financial Self-Sufficiency Evaluation Report which will be the basis in assessing whether the LEE has achieved financial self- sufficiency (or 100% cost recovery) and recommending whether the LEE shall continue to operate and related actions.
We also recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a complete set of financial statements for the Slaughterhouse every year that will be helpful in the proper assessment, monitoring and evaluation of its operations.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:

Adopt the accrual basis of accounting by recording on-going projects as of year-end based on the work accomplishment or percentage of completion as indicated in the Report on GPPA regardless of when payment was made. Ensure that the Report on GPPA at year-end is supported by Inspection Reports from the Municipal Engineering Office; and

Make the necessary adjusting entries to record the five ongoing projects and one completed project with the amounts of ₱8,643,664.13 and ₱986,826.51 by debiting the appropriate asset accounts and crediting the Accounts Payable account.

We recommended that Management direct the General Services Officer to:

For the ensuing procurement of goods of the LGU, we recommended that Management require the GSO and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to:
