observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate the necessary supporting documents in order to determine the exact/actual date of the receivable accounts  and to identify the LGUs and NGAs, and individuals/entities obliged to settle their obligations/debts with the LGU in order to establish the validity and accuracy of   the respective receivable account balances presented in the financial statements;
Send Confirmation and demand letters to identified employees, individuals and offices that have existing accounts with the LGU and require them to settle their obligations.
Observe the guidelines and procedures in requesting write-off of accounts as provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 when these accounts are confirmed to be non-moving for ten (10) years or more, and are considered to be dormant accounts.
Management commented that the Municipal Accountant was instructed to comply with the foregoing recommendations of the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Acting Municipal Treasurer and disbursing Officer to strictly adhere to the provisions in the granting and liquidation of cash advance as provided in COA Circular No. 97-002. For fair presentation of expense accounts and assets accounts in the year-end financial statements, ensure that all cash advances are liquidated at the end of the year.
We also recommended that when the beneficiaries were of a significant number, it is encourage that they shall be grouped or clustered, and cash advances shall be granted and liquidated accordingly to ensure that government funds are not expose to possible risk of loss through theft or misappropriation.
Management commented that cash advances under payroll fund account with a year-end balance of ₱5,796,255.60 were already fully liquidated as of January 28, 2021. Likewise, Management ensured that COA Circular No. 97-002 shall be strictly followed in granting and in the liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant and GSO to maintain and update the ledger cards and stock cards, respectively to be reconciled periodically pursuant to Sections 119, 120 and 124, Volume I of MNGAS to come up with a reliable and fair presentation of inventory accounts in the financial statements;
Instruct the GSO to ensure that physical inventory of supplies be conducted on inventory items every semester and the result of the physical count be reported in the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) which shall be submitted to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semester; and
Require the Municipal Accountant and GSO to reconcile the balances of inventory accounts as of December 31, 2021, and effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts by the preparation of a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) duly supported by Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) and Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) to take up any unrecorded issuance of supplies and materials.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the Municipal Assessor to submit a progress report  for the titling of 12 parcels of land considering that these were already in process since 2011, and to submit updates on the court litigation of the nine (9) parcels of land;
Require the Municipal Budget Officer to provide an appropriation for titling of parcels of land in the annual budget of the LGU;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Assessor to submit explanations/justifications why the Land account valued at ₱59,676,970.00 was based on Market Value instead of the acquisition cost of the land; and
Seek advice from the consultant/s of the LGU who are knowledgeable on this issue particularly on nine (9) parcels of land under court litigation.
Management commented that a memorandum was issued to the Municipal Assessor to submit progress report for the titling of 12 parcels of land and to submit updates on court litigation of the nine (9) parcels of land.  Appropriation for titling of parcels of land was included in the Annual Budget 2021 and 2022 in the amount of ₱500,000.00 under Legal services.
Management also commented that the valuation of Land was based on Market Value due to the absence of basis for the acquisition cost.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.
We also recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for road and road components for every road network, and disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the total road network system;
Require the General Services Officer, to maintain a Report of the Local Road Network and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components;
Direct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer with a complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects; and
Require the inventory committee to conduct physical count of a local road networks and to prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System to be reconciled with the accounting records.
Management commented that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and General Service Officer (GSO) are now complying on the recommendations of the Auditor. The Audit Team was informed that subsidiary ledger is maintained in the ECPAC System used by the Accounting Office. The Road Network was disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements. The Property Card for Local Road Network preparation is in progress. Report of Local Road Network was already submitted by the Municipal Engineer to the GSO and Municipal Accountant for the reconciliation. The conduct of the physical count of local road network is scheduled by the inventory committee on July 2022.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to transfer the unexpended balances of the QRF and Mitigation Fund-MOOE in the total amount of ₱3,024,185.56 from the General Fund to the Trust Fund under Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities of the LDRRMC within the next five years.
Management ensured to comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to verify the journal entries in the recording of the Due from/Due to Other Funds accounts and reconcile the two accounts accordingly.
Management commented that the unreconciled balance of ₱72,630.10 was presented in the notes to financial statements in 2021. These are checks dated December 31, 2021 representing a fund transfer awaiting deposit for the remittance of withholding tax under the Trust Fund account. The Audit Team was informed that the said variance was corrected upon deposit of the said checks on the month following.

We recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity:
Instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly observe the provisions set forth in the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 wherein competitive bidding shall be adopted. Other modes of procurement shall be resorted only if the given conditions are met;
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents to establish the validity and propriety of the transaction; and
Require the BAC and Administrative Officer IV to submit comments/justifications on the deficiencies noted.
Management commented that the Administrative Officer IV will submit the duly signed list of recipients.
A sudden surge of Covid-19 cases have prompted the BAC to submit the Request for Quotation (RFQ) in computerized form. Management assured that the BAC will not pre-determine prices as the committee knows very well that the RFQ is an obligation of the interested supplier/s.
Management also justified that the PhilGEPS System was unavailable during the month of procurement probably due to system maintenance. Instead, the BAC posted in conspicuous places the invitation to submit bids and proposals.
With regard to the procurement of gift checks, Management explained that in order to cope up with the stern virus of COVID-19, the LGU of Mangaldan thru its HoPE has to consider the basic needs of its employees. Hence, the ONE THOUSAND PESOS (₱1,000) in the form of GC given to all employees is reasonable in all its sense because not two (2) employees have the same needs and priorities at a given time. The GCs can help employees buy their immediate or urgent needs.
It was also explained that the BAC in good faith believed that the direct procurement was applicable to the subject AOM as when the whole nation was badly hit by the pandemic, the LGU of Mangaldan was not spared, and that during that time the only company offering gift certificate was the Magic Grocery in Mangaldan. The BAC had decided to purchase the Gift Certificates aftermarket scanning thru inquiries in nearby groceries in Mangaldan as well as in nearby towns and cities.
The Management respectfully and humbly requested the Audit Team to consider the aforementioned justifications in the spirit of humanity and fairness. Audit Team was assured that the BAC will certainly follow the recommendations by strictly observing the provisions of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to require the suppliers to strictly observe the BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 2-2014 on the issuance of Sales Invoice on various procurement of goods.
Management commented that the Acting Municipal Treasurer was instructed to strictly observe the BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 2-2014 on the issuance of Sales Invoice on various procurement of goods.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Engineer to prepare and submit duly approved Program of Work to the Audit Team which shall serve as standard/benchmark to support the reasonableness of the cost of the project, required time frame, and the required specifications in the implementation of the project. Strict compliance with the above-cited regulations is further recommended to avoid suspension in audit of the transaction.
Management commented that the Municipal Engineer was instructed to submit the Approved Program of Work of the said projects.

We recommended Management the following courses of action:
Limit the procurement of goods and services on a reimbursement basis where the circumstances warrant its application;
Require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to adhere strictly to the provision that all payments shall be made by check directly payable to the suppliers; and
Instruct the BAC to adopt public bidding or alternative modes of procurement provided under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to promote transparency and to avoid possible source of irregularities.
Management signified its willingness to comply with the audit recommendations. A memorandum was issued by the Municipal Mayor to set a limit in the procurement of goods and services on a reimbursement basis where the circumstances warrant its application. Persons responsible were also instructed to strictly comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team.

To control prices in the procurement of goods, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the End User/s or the implementing units to conduct market research related to prices of goods in the preparation of Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) which shall be the basis of the Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) where goods shall not be procured above the ABC. Ensure that goods are procured at a price most advantageous to the government.
Management commented that a memorandum will be issued to all employees/ end users to conduct market research related to prices of goods in the preparation of Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) which shall be the basis of the Approved Budget for the Contract to ensure that goods are procured at a price most advantageous to the government.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management release the shares of the barangays from the basic real property tax earnings on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter to enable the barangays to use the additional income for development projects beneficial to the barangay folks.
Management commented that the quarterly share of barangay is consistently prepared within five days after the quarter, however; cut-off for the share is behind by one month due to manual reconciliation of Accounting and Treasury. Audit recommendations are well-taken and management will do its best to adhere with the prescribed rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management undertake the following actions in the disposal of unserviceable/obsolete property and equipment, to wit:
Direct the GSO to report all unserviceable property and equipment of the LGU in the IIRUP and to ensure that the said report be duly accomplished;
Create a Disposal/Appraisal Committee to conduct and manage the disposal process; and
Require the GSO to ensure that the unserviceable/obsolete property and equipment be disposed pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445.
Management commented that they already taken steps in the proposed disposal of the LGU’s unserviceable vehicles. The Executive Order creating the Disposal and Appraisal Committee will be submitted. The GSO will comply with the procedures presented under Sec. 79 of PD 1445.

To strengthen the internal control in the use of government vehicles and to regulate fuel consumption of the LGU, we recommended that Management:
Submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Official Fuel Consumption to determine the reasonableness of gasoline consumption;
Ensure that each travel is supported by a duly accomplished, and duly signed and approved Driver’s Trip Ticket; and
Strictly observe the policies and guidelines provided in the provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61 for proper control and monitoring of vehicle utilization and gasoline consumption.
Management ensured to submit the Monthly Report of Official Travel and Fuel Consumption of the municipal government. As part of the GSO Personnel Program, municipal drivers were already trained on how to fill-out Driver’s Trip Tickets; hence, this is already being implemented by the management since 2019. Moreover, starting June 2021, management thru the GSO has been preparing Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Official Travel.
While it is true that the LGU has implemented a control mechanism in the utilization of motor vehicles since 2019, the LGU uses the Motor Vehicle Utilization Form instead of the prescribed Driver’s Trip Ticket. Likewise, validation of compliance on the submission of said reports disclosed that the LGU prepared the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Report of Fuel Consumption for the period June to December 2021 and was only received by this Office on March 30, 2022.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations in the proper handling and collection and ensure that all collections of municipal personnel assigned to collect taxes and fees are remitted daily to safeguard government funds from risk of loss, misuse or misappropriation of government funds.
We also recommended that Management instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to require the personnel concerned to remit immediately her collection in the amount of ₱31,339.02.
Management signified its willingness to comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that electronic or computerized system in assessment and collection of taxes and fees be considered and adopted by the LGU which is cost efficient in processing financial transactions. It will also facilitate the preparation of reports which are accurate and reliable. Furthermore, it will prevent/minimize errors and inconsistencies in the assessment and collection of taxes and other revenues of the LGU and will improve or increase the collection of the LGU.
Management commented that the Local Finance Committee together with Municipal Assessor are still scouting for an effective and efficient system provider that can deliver the services such as to generate regular reports required by various agencies of the government. The Audit Team was also informed that a budget of ₱3,000,000.00 was already allocated for the purpose.

We recommended to the Local School Board to include allocations from its SEF in support of the ECCD Program not only in compliance with RA No. 10410 and of DepED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017, but also to ensure that the preschoolers and their parents shall enjoy the benefit from the said program.
Management commented that the LSB PLAN 2022 duly approved by Sangguniang Bayan showed the inclusion of ECCD in the Local School Board Plan. Management ensured that the audit recommendation shall be fully complied with.

We recommended that Management require the current Municipal School Board to reorganize the composition of the School Board and to include the duly elected representative of the Teacher’s Organization for proper representation of teachers in the Municipality.
Management commented that the composition of the newly reorganized Local School Board with a representative from the Teachers’ Organization was already submitted in compliance with the AOM.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to intensify the collection of delinquent realty taxes by sending notice of delinquency and requesting the assistance of barangay officials to encourage the realty taxpayers to pay their real property taxes.  In case, the long outstanding tax delinquencies could not be collected, Management through the Acting Municipal Treasurer consider to apply the provisions of Sections 254 and Section 174, Chapter IV of RA No. 7160  in order to generate additional income for the financial operation of the LGU.
Management commented that despite the pandemic which was encountered from 2020 to present, the LGU still issued Demand Letters to Delinquent Real Property Taxpayers.  Management ensured to exert more effort this year 2022 to collect from delinquent taxpayers.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer and Market Supervisor to exert more effort to collect the delinquent market stall rentals from stallholders by adopting an effective collection strategy in order to generate more income which could be used in the implementation of development programs/projects beneficial to the constituents.
Management commented that at the start of CY 2022, market collectors exerted all available avenues and reasonable diligence to perform their duties and responsibilities that yielded positive results in reducing market stall rentals delinquencies by more than 33%.
It was also mentioned in their reply that based on the records, a huge amount of stall rental delinquencies are attributed mostly on the stalls awarded at the circular building; hence, most did not pay any stall rental from the time it was awarded. Further, all other delinquent stalls at public market have stopped operation causing stall rentals to freeze. Furthermore, some stallholders no longer exist and others were not interested to return to operate again.
In view of negligence of delinquent stallholders, Management may cause to recommend to the Market Committee headed by the Municipal Mayor in due time for the full implementation of existing provisions of local ordinances to collect and / or to award the same stalls to interested stakeholders.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with GAD Focal Person, to refrain from charging expenses that are not related in addressing gender issues, promote women’s empowerment, and gender equality as provided in the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, and to charge these expenses to other programs of the LGU where deemed appropriate;
Direct the GAD Focal Point to determine GAD related issues in the locality which is necessary in the formulation of GPB of the LGU and to strictly monitor the utilization of the fund in accordance with the programs, projects and activities in the GPB;
Require the Municipal Budget Officer and GAD Focal Point to submit written justifications/explanations why expenditures not related to the objectives of GAD and not included in the GPB were charged to GAD Fund instead to other programs of the LGU.
Management explained that the Mangaldan GAD Plan and Budget for CY 2021 is duly endorsed by the DILG - Pangasinan Provincial Office. The GAD Expenditures were aligned according to the GAD Plan and Budget.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
Section 36, Chapter VI of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act No. 9710 provides that the Commission on Audit shall conduct an annual audit on the use of the GAD budget for the purpose of determining its judicious use and the efficiency, and effectiveness of interventions in addressing gender issues towards the realization of the country’s commitments, plans and policies on women empowerment, gender equality, and GAD.   In view thereof, the Audit Team is not precluded to conduct an audit in the utilization of GAD funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point System to:
Institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender analysis, planning, programming and policy formulation;
Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the said issues; and
Revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, which identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.
Management commented that the GAD Database Updating with Gender Analysis has already been institutionalized in LGU Mangaldan the updated GAD DATABASE for CY 2021 with gender analysis was already submitted in compliance with the AOM issued.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure to implement programs, projects and activities (PPAs) under LCPC in accordance with the provision of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to address the needs of children and other related issues which needs immediate attention and action.
We also recommended to encourage the members of the LCPC to prepare Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children, and maximize the allocated LCPC Fund to identified PPAs for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries and the objectives of the law creating the LCPC fund shall be fully attained.
Management commented that they are now complying with the Memorandum Circular of DILG as to the manner of charging of expenses under the LCPC. The copy of Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children will be submitted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer to ensure that the LDRRMF are used to support disaster risk reduction and management projects and activities in compliance with the provisions of DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 13-1 and other pertinent provisions of related laws and its implementing rules and regulations in order to strengthen the capacity of the LGU to build the disaster resilience of communities, institutionalize measures for reducing disaster risks and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities in the locality.
We also recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer to stop the practice of charging expenditures against the LDRRMF which are not directly supportive in the implementation of disaster risk management programs and activities.
Management ensured that due to the exigency of times, the Management thru the reasonable and diligent discretion of the budget office and local disaster office deemed it necessary to charge the items in the said Appropriation. Nevertheless, Management ensured to comply with the recommendations of the audit team as to charging against the 70% pre disaster and 30% actual disaster.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive undertake the following courses of actions:
Require the former Acting Municipal Treasurer to immediately liquidate the cash advance for Payroll Fund amounting to ₱6,682,361.80 and adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 in granting and liquidation of cash advance.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Acting Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all cash advances be liquidated at the end of the year, or as soon as the purpose for which it was given has been served.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Assessor to:
Submit a progress report  for the titling of 12 parcels of land considering that these were already in process since CY 2011;
Submit updates on the court litigation of nine (9) parcels of land.
Ensure that a budget shall be allocated for land titling.

To address this perennial issue on doubtful presentation of PPE accounts in the financial statement, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the General Service Officer,  the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee to comply strictly with   COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts  of the LGU in order to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Market Supervisor to prepare regularly a monthly billing statements to the delinquent stallholders. A summary thereof shall be regularly submitted to the Acting Municipal Treasurer, and copy furnish the Municipal Accountant to recognize income when earned, whether collected or not.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to adhere with the accrual method of accounting pursuant to Paragraph 19 of IPSAS No. 9 and pertinent provision of GAM for LGUs. Thus, the uncollected rental from stallholders in the amount of ₱1,625,990.60 be recognized in the books as a debit to Operating Lease Receivable and Credit to Rent Income.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the BAC Secretariat to post all NOA, contract, or purchase order in the website of the LGU, if any, or at any conspicuous place in the premises of the PE and GPPB online portal for Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act for information purposes and to require the submission of the mandatory documentary requirements from the suppliers concerned;
Require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer and Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer to submit the list of affected families and proof of receipt of distribution of relief goods and other supporting documents and to submit comments/justifications on the deficiencies noted; and
Instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to require the suppliers to issue sales invoice upon payment of goods by the LGU pursuant to the provisions of Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 2-2014.
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to submit lacking documents to the Audit Team to ensure the validity and propriety of the claims on hazard pay.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of granting Transportation Allowance (TA) to officials and employees with assigned government vehicle/s or are presently using government motor transportation pursuant to Section 8.2.1 of Local Budget Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2013.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Engineer to submit complete detailed as-built plan which included the plan of the existing structure and the part that was actually built   to complete the technical evaluation of the COA TAS to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension.
Require the Contractor to refund the deficiency amounting to ₱929,326.03, and also impose the payment of liquidated damages due to incurrence of delay of 14 days, to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.
Require the Municipal Engineer to explain the  discrepancies  noted in the structural steel works under pay Item 403-a.
Instruct the Municipal Engineer to review the details of Statement of Work Accomplished against the actual accomplishment to ensure that projects were completed in accordance with the program of work to avoid audit disallowance on succeeding projects.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Require the Contractor to refund the deficiency amounting to ₱2,054,568.21 to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance; and
Instruct the Municipal Engineer to review the details of Statement of Work Accomplished against the actual accomplishment to ensure that projects were completed in accordance with the program of work to avoid audit disallowance on succeeding projects.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the BAC Chairman, BAC Secretariat, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents to facilitate the completion of the inspection report of the project.   We also recommended that the Management require the Contractor concerned to correct and eliminate the large volume of seepage in the pool.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management require the Contractor to refund the deficiency amounting to ₱3,794,958.37 and ₱2,236,573.93, respectively, to avoid the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Explain or justify the stoppage or suspension on the implementation of the construction of swimming pool/renovation of MYDC;
Provide this Office a legal basis to stop or suspend the said project; and
Institute the necessary remedies provided under Section 68 of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Resolution No. 05-2019, if warranted, such as, but not limited to the imposition of liquidated damages and rescission/ termination of projects with negative slippage of 15% or more.

We recommended that  Management stop the practice of using government funds to pay for the services of private lawyers and seek legal assistance from the Provincial Legal Office or Prosecutor’s Office in compliance with the provisions of Section 481 of RA No. 7160 and Revised Guidelines on the Deputation of Private Lawyers and Special Attorneys dated December 15, 2015.
We also recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and the Consultants to submit their monthly accomplisment reports for the month of January, February, July, September, November and December 2020 to support the claim for honorarium.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive stop the granting of honoraria to the four RTC Judges of Dagupan City pursuant to DBM Compensation Policy Guidelines No. 98-1 dated March 23, 1998.

In view of the foregoing, we recommend that Management require the BAC, BAC Secretariat and Municipal Accountant to submit the documentary requirements to support the payment of BAC honorarium.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all disbursements are duly supported with documentation in compliance with the provision of Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the charging of previous year’s expenditures to current appropriation pursuant to Section 350 of RA No. 7160.
Set a deadline for the submission of all claims of officials and employees, and creditors before the closing of books so that all unpaid obligations at the end of the year shall be recognized as expenditure on the year they are actually incurred.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to require the suppliers to issue Charge Sales Invoice for procurement on account. And when the goods are paid, the corresponding Collection Receipt shall be required as evidence of payment by the LGU.

We recommended that management release the basic RPT shares of barangays   within five days after the end of each quarter pursuant to the provision of Section 271 of Republic Act No. 7160, for a timely use of funds to support additional projects or programs of the barangays.

We recommended that policies and guidelines under COA Circular No. 77-61 be given due regard for proper control and monitoring of vehicle utilization and gasoline consumption which included the submission of Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption at the end of every month by using the prescribed format provided by the Audit Team for ready reference.
It is further recommended that Management require that all Driver’s Trip Ticket are properly numbered and recorded in a logbook for control purposes, and maintain a separate file of  Driver’s Trip Tickets for each vehicle for easy monitoring.

We recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare the Procurement Monitoring Report and approve by him and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and latest GPPB issuances.

We have recommended that Management instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to:
Comply with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations in the proper handling and collection of cash and ensure that all collections of municipal personnel assigned to collect taxes and fees are remitted daily to safeguard government funds from risk of loss, misuse or misappropriation of government funds;
Ensure the immediate remittance of ₱31,339.02 from the previous years collection of a former staff of the Municipal Treasury Office.
Ensure that check releases  be properly acknowledged by the claimants or by their representatives in the Check Register; and
Require the Disbursing Officer to maintain an updated cashbook that will be reconciled with the accounting records at least quarterly.

In view of the above, we reiterate our audit recommendation that electronic or computerized system in assessment and collection be considered and adopted by the LGU of Mangaldan which is a time and cost efficient scheme in processing transactions. It will also facilitate the preparation of reports which are accurate and reliable. Furthermore, it will prevent/minimize errors and inconsistencies in the assessment and collection of taxes and other revenues of the Municipality, whether intentional or not, thus, it will improve or increase the collection of the LGU. Therefore, the cost of said computerization program is considered in the planning and budgeting of LGU’s expenditures.

We reiterate the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to intensify the collection of delinquent realty taxes by sending notice of delinquency and requesting the assistance of barangay officials to encourage the realty taxpayers to pay their real property taxes.  In case of non-collection of  the long outstanding tax delinquencies, Management through the Municipal Treasurer consider to apply the provisions of Sections 254 and Section 174, Chapter IV of RA No. 7160  in order to generate additional income for the financial operation of the LGU.
We also recommended that records of the Municipal Treasury Office on delinquent RPT and SET be reconciled with the records of the Municipal Accounting Office.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer and Market Supervisor to consider an effective collection strategy in the collection of delinquent market stall rentals in order to generate additional income for the LGU.

We recommended that Management instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to:
Remit immediately the amount of unremitted taxes pertaining to CY 2020 in the amount of ₱115,815.93.
Ensure that all subsequent withholding of taxes be remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period to avoid imposition of fines and penalties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant, in cooperation with the Acting Municipal Treasurer, to cause the immediate remittance of the premiums/contributions totaling ₱52,757.30 to the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG to ensure that municipal officials and employees shall enjoy the services and benefits offered by the said government institutions and to avoid the payment of interest and penalties that will be imposed.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Budget Officer, in coordination with the GAD Focal Person to refrain from charging expenses that do not addressed gender issues, promote women’s empowerment and gender equality, pursuant to the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01, and ensure that the foregoing expenses are charged to other programs of the LGU where deemed appropriate.
We also recommended that the GAD Focal Point determine GAD related issues in the locality which is necessary in the formulation of GAD Plan and Budget of the LGU.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure to implement programs, projects and activities (PPAs) under LCPC in accordance with the provision of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 to address the needs of children and other related issues which needs immediate attention and action.
We also recommended to encourage the members of the LCPC to prepare Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children, and maximize the allocated LCPC Fund through identification of PPAs that will surely benefit the intended beneficiaries to attain the objectives of the law creating the LCPC fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer to ensure that the LDRRMF are used to support disaster risk reduction and management projects and activities in compliance with the provisions of DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 13-1 and other pertinent provisions of related laws and its implementing rules and regulations in order to strengthen the capacity of the LGU to build the disaster resilience of communities, institutionalize measures for reducing disaster risks and enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities in the locality.
We also recommended that Management require the Municipal Budget Officer to stop charging expenditures against the LDRRMF which are not directly supportive in the implementation of disaster risk management programs and activities.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive: 

a.	Instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain individual subsidiary ledger for all accounts, as required under Sections 111 and 114 PD No. 1445;



b.	Require the Municipal Accountant to:

 (1) locate the necessary supporting documents in order to determine the exact/actual date of the receivable accounts, and 



(2)  identify the LGUs and NGAs, and individuals/entities obliged to liquidate/settle the receivables to establish the validity of various receivable account balances presented in the financial statements;

c.	Observe the guidelines and procedures in requesting write-off of accounts as provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 when these accounts are confirmed to be non-moving for ten (10) years or more, and are considered to be dormant accounts; and  

d.	Require the Municipal Accountant to verify the journal entries made which were erroneously classified, and effect adjustments accordingly in the books of accounts.

Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all liquidations of cash advances are duly supported with complete documentation in compliance with Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the employee concerned to submit the necessary supporting documents such as copy of list of recipients with their signatures, official receipts, in case the amount was used to purchase goods for distribution to affected families of the Habagat and Typhoon Josie, as well as copy of the Declaration of State of Calamity by the Sangguniang Bayan of the Municipality or the Sangguniang Panlalawigan of the Province of Pangasinan, otherwise this transaction will be disallowed.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to process only those disbursement vouchers with complete documentation pursuant to the provision of Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.  

It is also recommended that the SB officials strictly comply with Section 4(6) of PD 1445, Section 447 (a) of RA No. 7160 and COA Circular 2017. Pursuant to this provision, it is recommended that the SBs refund the amount received.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor, being a recipient of a monthly cell card allowance of ₱8,000.00, refund the amount of ₱440,339.02 representing 15 postpaid accounts as these are considered excessive and unconscionable claim, otherwise, a Notice of Disallowance shall be issued.

Also, a legal basis  be presented to justify the hiring of retired public official despite the one year prohibition period was not yet ended, otherwise, all payments made be subjected to disallowance.

Direct the BAC-TWG to submit explanation on why the following deficiencies were disregarded during the post qualification evaluation, to wit:

Error in the computation of the Individual Program of work prepared by Safeway Construction; and

observations.recommendation:
Considering the material effect of the doubtful accuracy, validity and existence of
the property, plant and equipment in the fair presentation of the financial
statements of the LGU, we are reiterated our previous years audit
recommendation to strengthen the internal control system of the LGU in property
management, to wit:
a. Submit a copy of office order on the creation of Inventory Committee
to conduct physical count of properties of the municipality. Be
reminded that actual count could be undertaken any month during the
current year, but it has to be updated at year-end. A copy of the
Inventory Report has to be submitted to COA not later than January 31of
each year.
b. Reconcile the PPE per inventory report with accounting records/reports.
Any discrepancies shall be verified and adjusted accordingly. Recipient
of any missing PPEs be held accountable.
c. Maintain/update the PPE ledger cards and Property Card.
d. Maintain/update the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR). It has
to be renewed every three years or every time there is a change in
custodianship/user of the property.
e. Affix property tags to each property and equipment during the physical
inventory-taking for proper control and monitoring.
f. Make use of Invenory Custodian Slip (ICS) in the issuance of semi-
expendable items (such as punchers, staplers, scissors, etc,) for proper
accountability and control.
g. During the physical inventory, ensure that all identified unserviceable
property and equipment be reported in the Inventory and Inspection of
Report of Unserviceable Property which is the basis of the Accountant to
drop the unserviceable items from the respective PPE accounts and
reclassify the same to Other Assets account pending its disposal.
h. Submit Monthly Report on PPE Purchases with the following data to be
prepared by a designated Property Officer.
Reference PO No./ Acquisition Item Description Qty. Unit Cost Total
Date Check No. Date Date Cost
i. Submit Monthly Report on Semi-Expendable Purchases with the
following data to be prepared by a designated Supply Officer.
Reference PO No./ Acquisition Item Qty. Unit Cost Total Cost
Date Check No. Date Date

Provisions (Chapter VIII) of the Manual on Real Property Appraisal &
Assessment Operations dated January 2006, to wit:
Chairperson : Municipal Assessor

Member : Municipal Treasurer

dated October 19, 2009, Department of Finance, “Prescribing the Philippine
Valuation Standard.”

for adjustment on the valuation of Land account in the absence of historical
cost.

We have recommended that management continue to deduct premiums from the salaries and wages of LGU- Mangaldan employees and to provide the corresponding  government share, and remit the same with the GSIS pursuant to RA No. 8291 for the social security protections of government workers and employees.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the inventory team created by the agency have to complete the physical count of PPE and submit the Report on the Physical Count  of PPE not later than January 31 of each year.  Likewise, the Accounting Office and the General Services Office should prioritize the reconciliation of Report on the Physical Count of PPE with the accounting records/reports.
observations.managementAction: Submitted inventory on PPE as of January 2017. However, as per validation, there was no  reconciliation  with accounting department.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer exert effort to collect the amount due from the delinquent taxpayers. Formulate plans and adopt policies and strategies by taking into consideration the legal remedies provided under section 174 of RA 7160 to minimize delinquent taxes, if not totally eliminated
observations.managementAction: The Municipal treasurer/Land Tax Sector exert effort to collect the amount due from the delinquent taxpayers.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management give priority to install the solar lights project so that it could be utilized and served its intended purpose on time.
observations.managementAction: The solar street lights have been installed  at the Brgy. Embarcadero Bridge (8 units) and Brgy. Anolid  (4 units), Public Plaza(4 units) and the rest then is on-going installation at the various areas wherein lights are needed for public services.

observations.recommendation: We have recommend-ed that the Municipal Accountant strictly adhere with the provision of NGAS manual in the certification of financial transaction to accounts payable which should be done only upon receipts of goods and services and bills from the creditors.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant strictly adhere with the Provision of NGAS manual in the certification of Financial Transaction to Accounts Payable which should not be done only upon receipts of the goods and services and bills from the creditors.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management cause the immediate liquidation of all outstanding cash advances especially those outstanding for a long period of time. In case the officials and employees concerned fail to liquidate/settle, apply sanctions as provided under Section 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 8 Rule V of CSC Resolution No. 04-0676 dated June 14, 2004.
observations.managementAction: Management cause the immediate liquidation of all cash advances specially those outstanding for a long period of time. Refrain from granting cash advances to elected official other than their travelling expenses. Stop granting cash advances to Municipal Officials & Employees who still have previous/unliquidated cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We have recommend-ed that the Municipal Accountant verify the composition of the Due to and Due from Other Fund accounts and reconcile accord-ingly by preparing an adjusting journal entry for a fair and proper presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant verified the composition of the amounts actually due from/to other funds. Reconciliation made to include the necessary adjusting entries to ensure the accuracy of the accounts.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management comply with the monthly submission of Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF pursuant to Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 in order to monitor the propriety of the utilization of funds.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRM officer complied with the submission of Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF as provided in the aforementioned provisions in order to monitor compliance on accounting and reporting of the utilization of funds.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management adopt the Online Payroll System which is a time and cost efficient scheme of disbursing payroll to its employees. Likewise, it prevents possible risk such as robbery, hold-up and similar incidents
observations.managementAction: The Municipality adopted the online Payroll system which is a time and cost efficient  scheme of disbursing payroll officer to its employees, Likewise, it prevents possible risk such as robbery hold up and similar incidents.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit within the prescribed period of five (5) days copies of perfected contracts and supporting documents to COA auditor as required under COA Circular No. 2009-01 for a systematic and effective review process to come up with timely, relevant audit results.
observations.managementAction: The Local Chief Executive directed the Bids and Awards Committee/BAC Secretariat and the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit within the prescribed period of five (5) days copies of perfected contracts and supporting documents to COA  Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive should not  allow deductions from salaries accruing to employees which shall reduce the employee’s monthly net take home pay to an amount lower than what is  stated in the General Provisions of  the General Appropriation Act.  Effective January, 2016, the net take home pay should not be lowered than P3,500.00, so please be guided accordingly.
observations.managementAction: The Human Resource Management has issued a Memorandum (HR- Memorandum No. 2016-005) noted by the Local Chief Executive dated April 27, 2016 entitled “Disallowing Deductions from Salaries Accruing to Employees which shall reduce the Employees Monthly Net Take Home Pay to an amount lower than P3,500.00”.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry based on the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) and actual physical count of accountable forms as of a given period to reflect the correct valuation of said inventory account in the financial statement.
For the ensuing purchases of accountable forms, it is recommended that the  accountant recognize an inventory account and expense shall only be recognized on the cost of issued accountable forms.
observations.managementAction: The Accountant already recognized  an inventory account in the purchase of Accountable Form and expense was recognized on the issuance thereof.
